analysis.note: Instrumental governed by sahitam/saṃprayuktam: the afflictions with which the mind is conjoined.
Numeral qualifying kleśaiḥ; the commentary restricts the association to four kleśas, not all six.
Neuter nominative singular agreeing with the prior vijñānam/manas; the form is homophonous with accusative singular. The commentary glosses it as saṃprayuktam.
Qualifies kleśaiḥ: the four afflictions are both obscured/afflicted and ethically indeterminate.

verification.detail: compound stem nivṛtāvyākṛta as Pum/Trtiya/Bahu: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Glossed by the commentary as sarvakālam, ‘at all times’.

samasa.vigraha: ātmadṛṣṭiś cātmamohaś cātmamānaś cātmasnehaś ceti saṃjñitāḥ

analysis.note: Qualifies kleśaiḥ. The four internal ātmadṛṣṭi/ātmamoha/ātmamāna/ātmasneha terms are unpacked by the commentary as seeing, ignorance, conceit, and attachment/love with respect to self; the whole means ‘called by the names …’.

verification.detail: compound stem ātmadṛṣṭyātmamohātmamānātmasnehasaṃjñita as Pum/Trtiya/Bahu: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: Because verse 6 is elliptical, translate sahitam as predicated of the previously introduced manas/vijñāna, not of ālayavijñāna itself as the grammatical subject.
options_considered: make ālayavijñāna the grammatical subject of sahitam
leave the subject entirely unexpressed in English
supply the previously introduced mind/manas as subject
justifications.chosen: The supplied subject in translation is ‘this mind (manas)’, carried over from the previous verse.

evidence.quote:
āha ca
avidyayā cātmadṛṣṭyā cāsmimānena tṛṣṇayā
ebhiś caturbhiḥ saṃkliṣṭaṃ mananālakṣaṇaṃ manaḥ
evidence.translation: And he says: ‘By ignorance, by self-view, by I-conceit, and by craving—by these four—the mind whose characteristic is mentation is defiled.’

justifications.decision: The word excludes other caittas; it is not a general statement about all mental factors.
justifications.chosen: kleśaiḥ means ‘with afflictions’, a subset of mental factors.

evidence.quote:
caittā hi dviprakārāḥ kleśās tad anye ca
tadanyebhyo vyavacchedārtham āha
kleśair iti
evidence.translation: Mental factors are of two kinds: afflictions and the others. To exclude those others, he says ‘with afflictions.’

justifications.decision: Do not read this as a vague ‘fourfold’ epithet; it means four out of the six kleśas, since the mind is not associated with all six.
options_considered: four kinds of mental factors generally
justifications.chosen: caturbhiḥ restricts the kleśas to four.

evidence.quote:
kleśā api ṣaṭ
na ca taiḥ saṃprayujyate 'taś caturbhir ity āha
evidence.translation: The afflictions too are six; and it is not associated with those [all], therefore he says ‘with four.’

justifications.decision: The commentary glosses sahita directly with saṃprayukta, so it is stronger and more technical than loose accompaniment.
justifications.chosen: sahitam is translated as ‘conjoined/associated’ in the technical sense of saṃprayoga.

evidence.quote: yāvat tad asti tāvat taiḥ saṃprayuktam
evidence.translation: As long as it exists, it is associated with them.

justifications.decision: The four kleśas here are distinguished from akuśala kleśas; nivṛta is explained by being kliṣṭa, and avyākṛta by not being classed as wholesome or unwholesome.
justifications.chosen: nivṛtāvyākṛtaiḥ means ‘obscured-neutral’ or ‘afflicted yet ethically indeterminate’.

evidence.quote:
kleśā api dvividhāḥ
akuśalā nivṛtāvyākṛtāś ca
akuśalebhyo viśeṣārtham āha
nivṛtāvyākṛtair iti
evidence.translation: Afflictions too are of two kinds: unwholesome and obscured-neutral. To distinguish them from the unwholesome ones, he says ‘obscured-neutral.’

evidence.quote:
na hi nivṛtena vijñānenākuśalānāṃ saṃprayogaḥ saṃbhavati
nivṛtāḥ kliṣṭatvāt
avyākṛtāḥ kuśalākuśalatvenāvyākaraṇāt
evidence.translation: For there is no association of unwholesome [factors] with an obscured cognition. They are obscured because they are afflicted; they are indeterminate because they are not determined as wholesome or unwholesome.

justifications.decision: The scope is temporal and constant: whenever that mind exists, it is associated with these four.
justifications.chosen: sadā is translated as ‘always, so long as it exists’.

evidence.quote:
sadeti
sarvakālam
yāvat tad asti tāvat taiḥ saṃprayuktam
evidence.translation: ‘Always’ means ‘at all times’: as long as it exists, it is associated with them.

justifications.decision: The bhāṣya quotes ātmadṛṣṭi and glosses it by darśana and satkāyadṛṣṭi; the four names are self-view, delusion about self, self-conceit, and self-love/attachment.
options_considered: four named kleśas beginning with ātmadṛṣṭi, ‘self-view’
justifications.chosen: Read ātmadṛṣṭi, not ātmaduṣṭi, and take the final compound as naming four specific kleśas.

evidence.quote:
sāmānyanirdeśād viśeṣato na jñāyanta iti viśeṣato nirdiśati
ātmadṛṣṭyātmamohātmamānātmasnehasaṃjñitaiḥ
evidence.translation: Since from a general designation they are not known specifically, he states them specifically: ‘called self-view, self-delusion, self-conceit, and self-love.’

evidence.quote:
upādānaskandheṣv ātmeti darśanam ātmadṛṣṭiḥ satkāyadṛṣṭir ity arthaḥ
moho
'jñānam
ātmany ajñānam ātmamohaḥ
ātmaviṣaye māna ātmamāno 'smimāna ity
arthaḥ
ātmani sneha ātmasneha ātmapremety arthaḥ
evidence.translation: Seeing ‘self’ in the aggregates of clinging is self-view; the meaning is satkāyadṛṣṭi. Delusion is ignorance. Ignorance regarding self is self-delusion. Conceit with self as its object is self-conceit; the meaning is I-conceit. Affection/love toward self is self-love; the meaning is love for self.

justifications.decision: Avoid a translation that implies the text affirms an ātman; the commentary frames these as confusion, self-view, conceit, and attachment concerning what is taken to be self.
options_considered: translate ātma as a doctrinally real Self
take ātma merely as a neutral reflexive ‘own’
translate as a misconstrued/self-imagined object, here focused on ālayavijñāna
justifications.chosen: The ‘self’ in the four names is not an accepted real self; in this context it is an object imagined as self, especially with respect to ālayavijñāna.

evidence.quote:
tatrālayavijñānasvarūpe
saṃmūḍhaḥ sann ālayavijñāne ātmadṛṣṭim utpādayati
ātmadarśanād yā
cittasyonnatiḥ so 'smimānaḥ
etasmiṃs traye saty ātmābhimate vastuni yo
'bhiṣvaṅgaḥ sa ātmasnehaḥ
evidence.translation: There, with regard to the own-nature of ālayavijñāna, being deluded, one produces self-view toward ālayavijñāna. The elevation of mind that arises from seeing self is I-conceit. When these three are present, the clinging to an object conceived as self is self-love.

translation:
Always, so long as it exists, this [mind (manas)]
is conjoined (saṃprayukta) with four obscured-neutral afflictions
(kleśa; nivṛtāvyākṛta):
those called self-view (ātmadṛṣṭi), delusion about self (ātmamoha),
self-conceit (ātmamāna), and self-love/attachment (ātmasneha).
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind translation might render nivṛtāvyākṛtaiḥ from the dictionary as ‘restrained/unmanifest and unexpounded’; Sthiramati instead makes it the technical class ‘obscured-neutral’, i.e. afflicted yet not classed as wholesome or unwholesome (trbh 392–398).
It might take sahitam as loose ‘accompanied by’; the bhāṣya explicitly glosses it as saṃprayuktam, technical conjunction/association (trbh 391).
It might miss that caturbhiḥ means four out of the six kleśas, not all afflictions or a generic fourfoldness (trbh 389–390).
It might accept the vulgate ātmaduṣṭi or translate the long compound vaguely; the commentary reads ātmadṛṣṭi and defines all four names one by one (trbh 403–410).
It might take ‘always’ as rhetorical; Sthiramati specifies ‘at all times’ as ‘as long as it exists, it is associated with them’ (trbh 399–401).
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 split nivṛtāvyākṛtaiḥ into nivṛta + avyākṛtaiḥ and tagged nivṛta as a separate participial item; I analyze the bhāṣya reading as one karmadhāraya compound stem nivṛtāvyākṛta, instr. pl. masc., because the commentary quotes nivṛtāvyākṛtair iti at trbh 395 and defines nivṛtāḥ/avyākṛtāḥ as two properties of the same kleśas at trbh 397–398.

ByT5 supplied component Cp tags for the long final expression; I analyze ātmadṛṣṭyātmamohātmamānātmasnehasaṃjñitaiḥ as one inflected compound stem, instr. pl. masc., qualifying kleśaiḥ, because trbh 403 quotes it as the verse expression and trbh 404–410 unpack its four names.

If ByT5’s sahitam_SAM is taken as accusative singular, I construe sahitam contextually as neuter nominative singular with the prior vijñānam/manas; the surface form is nominative/accusative singular homophonous.
